enrichment.short_description: Free cloud-based time tracking and attendance software with GPS, facial recognition, and unlimited users.
enrichment.seo_title: Jibble Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Jibble review 2026: see how this free time tracking and attendance app uses GPS, facial recognition and biometrics, plus pricing tiers and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Jibble is a cloud-based time tracking and workforce attendance platform built around a permanently free core plan, positioning it as one of the most widely adopted 'free forever' time clocks on the market with a self-reported user base above 1.3 million. The company was founded in 2016 by Asim Qureshi and now operates as Jibble Group, headquartered in Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia, with a fully remote, globally distributed team of roughly 50 to 100 people. Jibble Group has raised approximately 7 million dollars in funding across angel and seed rounds from a syndicate of around 39 investors according to Crunchbase, and third-party revenue trackers report annual revenue in the range of 7 to 10.5 million dollars with tens of thousands of customers, including Accenture, OpenWater and PayrollPanda. Technically, Jibble runs as a browser-based web app backed by native mobile apps for iOS and Android, desktop apps for Mac and Windows, a Chrome extension, and integrations that let employees clock in and out directly from Slack or Microsoft Teams. A shared 'kiosk mode' turns any tablet or terminal into a physical time clock for on-site or shift-based teams, and the system supports offline clock-ins that automatically sync once connectivity returns. Its differentiating features center on fraud prevention: AI-powered facial recognition and selfie capture verify that the person clocking in is who they claim to be, GPS location stamping and geofencing confirm they are on-site, and NFC card and PIN options add further verification layers for construction, field service, retail, hospitality, healthcare and logistics employers who need to stop buddy punching. Beyond raw clock-in data, Jibble automates timesheet calculation including breaks and overtime rules, tracks paid time off and leave accrual, offers one-click timesheet approvals, generates exportable XLS and CSV reports and real-time attendance dashboards, and converts billable hours into client invoices, while also connecting to payroll and accounting systems such as QuickBooks, Xero and Deel. Pricing follows a freemium model: the Free plan is unlimited in user count with no credit card required and includes GPS tracking with two geofences, facial recognition, break and overtime tracking, project tracking and unlimited integrations; the Premium plan costs 4.49 dollars per user per month billed annually, after a March 2026 price increase from 2.49 dollars, and adds advanced geofencing plus activity screenshots; the Ultimate plan costs 7.99 dollars per user per month billed annually and unlocks biometric verification on every clock-in, advanced reporting and priority support; and an Enterprise tier offers custom pricing with dedicated onboarding and compliance features for large organizations. Every paid plan except Enterprise includes a 14-day free trial that does not require a credit card. Jibble's target audience skews toward small and mid-sized businesses and deskless, hourly or field-based workforces that need low-cost, high-trust attendance tracking rather than deep project-management functionality, though its project tracking also serves agencies billing hourly clients. In the crowded time-tracking category, Jibble competes most directly with Clockify, which is similarly free-forever and add-on based, Toggl Track, which emphasizes lightweight project time tracking for freelancers, TimeCamp, Deputy and When I Work, which lean harder into shift scheduling for hourly retail staff, and Connecteam, an all-in-one deskless workforce app. Jibble differentiates from these rivals primarily through its unusually generous unlimited-user free tier combined with biometric verification bundled even into that free plan, a combination most competitors reserve for paid tiers, making it a common recommendation for cost-sensitive SMBs and non-profits that still need fraud-resistant attendance data for payroll and compliance.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Jibble is a cloud-based time tracking and attendance platform aimed at businesses that need accurate, fraud-resistant clock-in data without paying per-user fees for basic tracking. Founded in 2016 by Asim Qureshi, the company has grown into Jibble Group, a remote-first organization headquartered in Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia, serving well over a million users across industries such as construction, retail, hospitality, healthcare and logistics.

Unlike many time-tracking tools that gate essential features behind a paywall, Jibble offers a genuinely free, unlimited-user plan that includes GPS tracking, facial recognition and automated timesheets. This positions it as an entry point for small businesses and non-profits, while its Premium and Ultimate paid tiers add deeper reporting, screenshots, and advanced verification for larger or compliance-sensitive teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Jibble's core strength is identity and location verification. AI-powered facial recognition and selfie capture confirm that the person clocking in is the actual employee, while GPS stamping and geofencing confirm they are physically at an approved work site, addressing the buddy-punching problem common with manual or PIN-only time clocks. A kiosk mode turns any shared tablet into a fixed time clock for on-site teams, and offline mode lets field workers clock in without signal, syncing automatically once back online.

Beyond clock-ins, Jibble automates timesheet calculations including breaks and overtime, manages leave and PTO accrual, and supports one-click approval workflows for managers. Reporting tools export to XLS and CSV, and the platform connects to payroll and accounting systems including QuickBooks, Xero and Deel, letting finance teams move directly from tracked hours to payroll runs without manual re-entry.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Jibble uses a freemium model anchored by a Free plan with no user limit, no credit card requirement, and no trial period restrictions — it includes GPS tracking with two geofences, facial recognition, break and overtime tracking, and unlimited integrations. Paid tiers unlock additional depth: Premium is 4.49 dollars per user per month (billed annually) and Ultimate is 7.99 dollars per user per month (billed annually), both up significantly from 2025 pricing after a March 2026 increase.

An Enterprise plan offers custom pricing for large organizations that need dedicated onboarding, single sign-on and compliance support. Every paid plan aside from Enterprise includes a 14-day free trial with no credit card required, making it low-risk for teams to test premium reporting and verification features before committing to a paid seat count.

features.title: GPS Time Tracking and Geofencing
features.description: Location-stamped clock-ins with configurable geofences confirm employees are on-site before a shift is logged.

features.title: Facial Recognition Verification
features.description: AI-based facial recognition and selfie capture prevent buddy punching by confirming employee identity at clock-in.

features.title: Automated Timesheets
features.description: Hours, breaks and overtime are calculated automatically and routed through one-click manager approval workflows.

features.title: Kiosk Mode
features.description: Any shared tablet or terminal can act as a physical time clock for on-site or shift-based teams.

features.title: Slack and Microsoft Teams Clock-In
features.description: Employees can clock in and out directly from chat tools without opening a separate app.

features.title: Leave and PTO Management
features.description: Tracks paid time off requests, approvals and accrual alongside regular time records.

features.title: Payroll Integrations
features.description: Native connections to QuickBooks, Xero and Deel move tracked hours directly into payroll processing.

features.title: Offline Mode
features.description: Field and remote employees can clock in without a signal, with entries syncing automatically once reconnected.

pros: Unlimited users on a genuinely free-forever plan with no credit card required
Biometric facial recognition and geofencing help prevent buddy punching even on the free tier
Works across web, iOS, Android, Mac, Windows and shared kiosk devices
Fast setup and simple interface suited to non-technical hourly workforces
Strong offline support for field, construction and remote teams
cons: Advanced reporting, screenshots and priority support require paid Premium or Ultimate tiers
Premium and Ultimate prices rose sharply in March 2026, roughly 60 to 80 percent higher than before
Less scheduling and shift-planning depth than dedicated workforce platforms like Deputy or When I Work
Some users report occasional GPS or facial recognition accuracy issues on older mobile devices
Support responsiveness can be slower for customers on the free plan

faqs.question: Is Jibble actually free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Jibble's Free plan supports an unlimited number of users with no credit card required, and includes GPS tracking, facial recognition and automated timesheets.

faqs.question: What does Jibble Premium add over the free plan?
faqs.answer: Premium, priced at 4.49 dollars per user per month billed annually, adds features such as advanced geofencing and activity screenshots on top of the free tier's core tracking tools.

faqs.question: Does Jibble work without an internet connection?
faqs.answer: Yes. Jibble supports offline clock-ins on mobile, and entries automatically sync to the account once the device reconnects to the internet.

faqs.question: How does Jibble prevent buddy punching?
faqs.answer: Jibble uses AI-powered facial recognition, selfie capture, GPS geofencing and optional NFC card or PIN verification to confirm the person clocking in is the actual employee at an approved location.

faqs.question: Does Jibble integrate with payroll software?
faqs.answer: Yes. Jibble connects directly to payroll and accounting platforms including QuickBooks, Xero and Deel to move tracked hours into payroll runs.

faqs.question: What is Jibble's kiosk mode used for?
faqs.answer: Kiosk mode turns a shared tablet or terminal into a physical time clock, letting multiple employees clock in and out from one shared device, commonly used in retail, hospitality and construction sites.

use_cases.title: Field service and construction attendance
use_cases.description: GPS-verified clock-ins and geofencing confirm crews are on the correct job site before hours are logged, reducing payroll disputes.

use_cases.title: Shift-based retail and hospitality teams
use_cases.description: Kiosk mode lets multiple hourly staff clock in on one shared device with facial recognition to prevent buddy punching.

use_cases.title: Remote and distributed agency billing
use_cases.description: Project-level time tracking and automated timesheets let agencies convert tracked hours directly into client invoices.
use_cases.audience: Remote agencies and professional services firms

enrichment.short_description: German website builder offering AI-assisted and drag-and-drop editors for small business sites and online stores.
enrichment.seo_title: Jimdo Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Jimdo review 2026: explore this Hamburg-based website builder's AI and drag-and-drop editors, e-commerce tools, pricing plans and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Jimdo is a German website builder that lets small businesses and freelancers create and manage websites and online stores without coding, offered through two distinct editors: Jimdo Creator, the original drag-and-drop builder, and Jimdo Dolphin, a newer AI-driven builder that generates a starting site from a short questionnaire about the business and then lets the owner refine it visually. The company traces its roots to a farmhouse near Cuxhaven in northern Germany, where Christian Springub and Fridtjof Detzner ran a small web design agency for local businesses starting around 2004; when Matthias Henze joined the team, the trio built their own content management system to speed up client work, which they launched publicly as Jimdo in 2007. Henze serves as CEO, with Springub and Detzner as co-founders. The company grew into a profitable, founder-led business headquartered in Hamburg, Germany, with additional offices in Munich and Tokyo, and today employs roughly 230 to 290 people across Europe, North America and Asia. In June 2015, Jimdo raised 25 million euros from US growth equity firm Spectrum Equity to fund international expansion, localizing the product into 11 languages and 20 currencies; notably, in 2024 the founders repurchased shares from Spectrum and other external investors, returning Jimdo to full founder ownership, an unusual trajectory in a website-builder market otherwise dominated by venture-backed or private-equity-owned players. Architecturally, Jimdo is a fully hosted SaaS platform: customers do not manage servers, and both Creator and Dolphin run in the browser with responsive, mobile-optimized output, integrated SSL, and built-in SEO tools such as automated meta tag suggestions, sitemap generation, and search-engine visibility scoring. E-commerce customers get inventory management, multiple payment gateway support, discount codes and product variants. Pricing is tiered and split between website plans and online store plans, all billed as annual or multi-month commitments rather than pure month-to-month. On the website side, the Play plan is free and limited to 5 pages and 500MB of storage; Start costs 11 dollars a month, includes a free domain for the first year, 5GB storage and up to 10 pages; Grow costs 17 dollars a month with 15GB storage, 50 pages and faster support; and Unlimited costs 45 dollars a month with unlimited storage and pages plus a professional design analysis. On the store side, Basic costs 18 dollars a month with a single shop design, Business costs 26 dollars a month and adds product variants and discounting, and VIP costs 45 dollars a month with premium support and unlimited subpages. Jimdo's core audience is small business owners, solopreneurs, freelancers and local service providers, particularly in the DACH region (Germany, Austria, Switzerland) where it holds strong brand recognition as a homegrown alternative to US platforms, alongside growing English-speaking and international user bases. Its principal competitors are Wix, Squarespace, WordPress.com, GoDaddy Website Builder, Weebly and, for e-commerce specifically, Shopify; Jimdo differentiates through its AI-first Dolphin onboarding flow that produces a usable site in minutes, its European data-hosting posture attractive to privacy-conscious customers, automated SEO tooling aimed at non-technical users, and its unusual status as a profitable, founder-owned company rather than a venture-backed or private-equity-controlled competitor.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Jimdo is a website builder built for people with no coding experience, offering two editing paths: Jimdo Creator, a traditional drag-and-drop builder, and Jimdo Dolphin, an AI-assisted builder that generates a working starter site after users answer a short set of questions about their business. Both products are aimed squarely at small businesses, freelancers and local service providers who need a professional web presence quickly.

Founded in 2007 in Hamburg, Germany, Jimdo has grown into a profitable, founder-owned company with offices in Munich and Tokyo. It occupies a distinct niche among website builders as a European, privacy-conscious alternative to US-based platforms like Wix and Squarespace, with particularly strong brand recognition in Germany, Austria and Switzerland.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Jimdo Dolphin's standout feature is its AI onboarding flow, which asks a handful of questions about the business type, style preferences and content needs, then assembles a starter site with relevant sections, imagery and copy suggestions that owners can edit visually. Jimdo Creator, by contrast, gives more granular manual control over layout via drag-and-drop blocks, suited to users who want to build from a blank canvas rather than an AI-generated draft.

Both editors include integrated SEO tools such as automated meta-tag suggestions and search-visibility scoring, mobile-responsive output by default, built-in SSL, and analytics dashboards. E-commerce customers get inventory tracking, multiple payment gateways, discount codes and product variant management, positioning the higher website and store tiers as a lightweight alternative to dedicated e-commerce platforms for small catalogs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Jimdo splits pricing into website plans and online store plans, both billed on annual or multi-month terms rather than flexible monthly billing. Website plans run from the free Play tier (5 pages, 500MB storage) through Start (11 dollars a month, free domain for year one), Grow (17 dollars a month, 50 pages) and Unlimited (45 dollars a month, unlimited pages and storage plus a professional design review).

Store plans start at Basic (18 dollars a month, single shop design), step up to Business (26 dollars a month, product variants and discount codes), and top out at VIP (45 dollars a month, premium support and unlimited subpages). A custom domain is included free for the first year on paid plans, after which renewal is billed separately, typically around 20 dollars a year.

features.title: AI Site Generation (Dolphin)
features.description: Answers to a short business questionnaire generate a ready-to-edit starter website with relevant sections and copy suggestions.

features.title: Drag-and-Drop Editor (Creator)
features.description: Manual block-based editing for users who want granular control over layout without AI assistance.

features.title: Integrated SEO Tools
features.description: Automated meta tag suggestions, sitemap generation and a search-visibility score to guide non-technical optimization.

features.title: E-commerce and Online Store
features.description: Inventory management, product variants, discount codes and multiple payment gateway support for small online shops.

features.title: Mobile-Responsive Design
features.description: All templates and AI-generated layouts render responsively across devices by default.

features.title: Built-In SSL and Hosting
features.description: Fully managed hosting with SSL certificates included on every paid plan, no server management required.

features.title: Multi-Language and Multi-Currency Support
features.description: Site content and checkout can be localized across roughly 11 languages and 20 currencies for international customers.

features.title: Custom Domain Included
features.description: Paid plans include a free custom domain for the first year, simplifying setup for new site owners.

pros: AI-assisted Dolphin builder produces a usable site in minutes for non-technical users
Strong built-in SEO guidance aimed specifically at small business owners
European-hosted, privacy-conscious alternative to major US website builders
Founder-owned and profitable, giving it product stability outside typical VC growth pressure
Free Play plan available with no time limit for very simple sites
cons: No true month-to-month billing; plans require annual or multi-month commitments
Fewer third-party app integrations and design templates than Wix or Squarespace
E-commerce features are lighter than dedicated platforms like Shopify for larger catalogs
Free Play plan is limited to 5 pages and 500MB of storage
Switching between Creator and Dolphin editors is not seamless if a business outgrows one

faqs.question: What is the difference between Jimdo Creator and Jimdo Dolphin?
faqs.answer: Creator is Jimdo's original manual drag-and-drop website builder, while Dolphin is a newer AI-assisted builder that generates a starter site from a short questionnaire before letting users refine it visually.

faqs.question: Is Jimdo free to use?
faqs.answer: Jimdo offers a free Play plan limited to 5 pages and 500MB of storage; paid plans start at 11 dollars a month for more pages, storage and a free first-year domain.

faqs.question: Can I sell products with Jimdo?
faqs.answer: Yes. Jimdo offers dedicated online store plans starting at 18 dollars a month with inventory management, product variants, discount codes and payment gateway support.

faqs.question: Where is Jimdo based?
faqs.answer: Jimdo is headquartered in Hamburg, Germany, with additional offices in Munich and Tokyo, and has been fully founder-owned since 2024.

faqs.question: Does Jimdo include SEO tools?
faqs.answer: Yes. Jimdo provides built-in SEO guidance including automated meta tag suggestions, sitemap generation and a search-visibility scoring tool aimed at non-technical users.

faqs.question: Can I use my own domain with Jimdo?
faqs.answer: Yes. Paid Jimdo plans include a custom domain free for the first year, after which it renews at a standard annual rate.

use_cases.title: Local service business websites
use_cases.description: Owners with no coding skills use Dolphin's AI questionnaire to launch a professional site with contact details, services and SEO basics in minutes.

use_cases.title: Small online stores
use_cases.description: Store plans handle inventory, product variants and payments for shops with modest catalogs that do not need a dedicated e-commerce platform.

use_cases.title: Portfolio and personal brand sites
use_cases.description: Creator's manual drag-and-drop editor suits designers, consultants and creatives who want full layout control.

enrichment.short_description: Conversation and revenue intelligence platform that records, transcribes and analyzes sales calls with AI.
enrichment.seo_title: Jiminny Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Jiminny review 2026: see how this London-based conversation intelligence platform records and analyzes sales calls with AI, plus pricing and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Jiminny is a conversation and revenue intelligence platform that records, transcribes and analyzes sales calls, video meetings and emails to give sales and customer success teams actionable coaching and pipeline insights. The company was founded in 2016 by Tom Lavery, who serves as CEO, alongside co-founders including Shelley Lavery, and is headquartered in London, United Kingdom, with additional presence in the United States to serve its growing North American customer base. Jiminny has raised roughly 19 million dollars in total funding, starting with a 2.5 million dollar seed round and followed by a 16.5 million dollar Series A in August 2022 led by growth equity firm Kennet Partners; the Series A was earmarked specifically to fund international expansion into the US market and deepen the product's AI capabilities. The company employs an estimated 51 to 200 people, with third-party trackers estimating annual recurring revenue in the low single-digit millions of dollars and roughly a thousand customers as of recent reporting. Architecturally, Jiminny is a cloud-based SaaS platform that plugs into a company's existing calling, video conferencing (such as Zoom and Google Meet) and CRM systems, using an AI notetaker to join and record calls, transcribe them, and extract structured data such as talk-to-listen ratios, competitor mentions, objection handling, and next-step commitments. That data feeds into a conversation intelligence layer that generates automated call summaries, syncs notes and action items directly into CRM systems like Salesforce and HubSpot, and surfaces coaching recommendations for managers, including searchable call libraries and moment-tagging so managers can build training clips from real customer conversations. A revenue intelligence layer aggregates this call-level data across the pipeline to flag at-risk deals, highlight competitive threats mentioned by prospects, and give sales leadership board-ready visibility into rep performance and deal health without manual reporting. The company is SOC 2 Type II certified and reports GDPR, CCPA and HIPAA compliance along with a 99.9 percent uptime guarantee, positioning it for regulated and enterprise buyers. Jiminny does not publish list pricing on its website; instead it sells through a seat-based model with three tiers reported by third-party pricing trackers: a free Listener seat for viewing shared recordings without recording capability, an Insights seat (reported around 42 dollars per user per month) for accessing dashboards and analytics without live call recording, and a Recording seat (reported around 83 dollars per user per month) that includes full call recording, transcription and coaching tools; annual contracts are typically required, with multi-year commitments earning discounts of roughly 15 to 20 percent, and an Enterprise tier available with custom pricing and dedicated support for larger deployments. Jiminny's core buyers are B2B sales and customer success leaders at small and mid-market companies who want conversation intelligence without the enterprise price tag of category leaders. Its primary competitors are Gong, the market-leading revenue intelligence platform known for its scale and AI depth; Chorus by ZoomInfo; Clari; and Wingman (Clari Copilot); Jiminny differentiates from these larger, pricier rivals by positioning itself as a more affordable, faster-to-deploy option aimed at growing sales teams rather than large enterprise revenue operations functions, while still offering SOC 2-certified security and CRM-native workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Jiminny is a conversation and revenue intelligence platform that records, transcribes and analyzes sales calls, video meetings and emails, turning raw customer conversations into structured coaching and pipeline insights for revenue teams. It joins calls automatically via an AI notetaker, syncs summaries and action items into the CRM, and surfaces trends across a team's full body of conversations.

Founded in 2016 by Tom Lavery and headquartered in London, Jiminny has grown from a UK-focused startup into a company serving B2B sales and customer success teams internationally, backed by roughly 19 million dollars in venture funding including a 16.5 million dollar Series A in 2022 aimed at expanding into the US market.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At the core of Jiminny is an AI notetaker that joins calls on platforms like Zoom and Google Meet, records and transcribes the conversation, and extracts structured signals such as talk-to-listen ratio, competitor mentions and objection handling. This data feeds a coaching layer that lets managers tag key moments, build searchable clip libraries for training, and score reps against custom scorecards.

Beyond individual call analysis, Jiminny's revenue intelligence layer rolls conversation data up across the pipeline to flag at-risk deals, highlight recurring objections and competitive mentions, and give leadership visibility into deal health and rep performance without manual reporting. CRM logging automatically syncs notes, next steps and deal updates into systems like Salesforce and HubSpot, reducing administrative work for reps.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Jiminny does not publish list pricing publicly, selling instead through custom quotes based on a mix of seat types. Reported pricing includes a free Listener seat for viewing shared recordings without recording capability, an Insights seat (around 42 dollars per user per month) for dashboard and analytics access without live call recording, and a Recording seat (around 83 dollars per user per month) that unlocks full recording, transcription and coaching features.

Most plans require annual contracts, with multi-year commitments typically earning discounts of 15 to 20 percent. Prospective customers can trial the platform for 14 days before committing, and an Enterprise tier is available with custom pricing, dedicated support and tailored security requirements for larger sales organizations.

features.title: AI Notetaker and Call Recording
features.description: Automatically joins and records calls across major video platforms, capturing audio, video and transcripts.

features.title: Conversation Intelligence
features.description: Extracts talk-to-listen ratios, competitor mentions, objection patterns and coaching-worthy moments from every call.

features.title: CRM Logging
features.description: Syncs call summaries, notes and next steps automatically into Salesforce, HubSpot and other CRM systems.

features.title: Sales Coaching Tools
features.description: Lets managers tag key call moments, build clip libraries and score reps against custom coaching scorecards.

features.title: Pipeline and Revenue Intelligence
features.description: Aggregates conversation data across the pipeline to flag at-risk deals and surface deal-winning behaviors.

features.title: Competitive Intelligence
features.description: Tracks and surfaces competitor mentions across calls to give sales and product teams real-time market visibility.

features.title: Searchable Call Library
features.description: Full-text searchable archive of past calls for onboarding, training and deal handoffs.

pros: Positioned as a more affordable alternative to enterprise-grade revenue intelligence platforms like Gong
Strong CRM-native workflow that logs notes and next steps automatically
SOC 2 Type II certified with GDPR, CCPA and HIPAA compliance for regulated buyers
Free Listener seat option lets non-recording stakeholders view shared call insights at no cost
Coaching and clip-library tools are well suited to structured sales enablement programs
cons: Pricing is not published; buyers must go through a sales process to get a quote
Annual contracts are typically required, limiting flexibility for smaller or newer teams
Recording seats needed for full functionality are priced well above the advertised entry point
Smaller AI research and feature-release pace compared to category leader Gong
Multi-year commitments carry lock-in risk if the platform does not deliver expected value

faqs.question: What does Jiminny do?
faqs.answer: Jiminny is a conversation and revenue intelligence platform that records, transcribes and analyzes sales calls, video meetings and emails to give sales teams coaching insights and pipeline visibility.

faqs.question: How much does Jiminny cost?
faqs.answer: Jiminny does not publish official pricing. Third-party trackers report an Insights seat around 42 dollars per user per month and a Recording seat around 83 dollars per user per month, both typically on annual contracts, alongside a free Listener seat.

faqs.question: Who founded Jiminny?
faqs.answer: Jiminny was founded in 2016 by Tom Lavery, who serves as CEO, alongside co-founders including Shelley Lavery.

faqs.question: Is Jiminny SOC 2 certified?
faqs.answer: Yes. Jiminny is SOC 2 Type II certified and reports compliance with GDPR, CCPA and HIPAA, along with a 99.9 percent uptime guarantee.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Jiminny's Insights and Recording seats?
faqs.answer: Insights seats provide access to dashboards, analytics and shared recordings but cannot record new calls. Recording seats include full call recording, transcription and coaching capabilities.

faqs.question: How does Jiminny compare to Gong?
faqs.answer: Jiminny targets small and mid-market sales teams with a lower-cost, faster-to-deploy alternative to Gong, which is the larger, more established revenue intelligence platform aimed at enterprise revenue operations teams.

use_cases.title: Sales call coaching
use_cases.description: Managers review AI-tagged call moments and build clip libraries to coach reps on objection handling and pitch delivery.

use_cases.title: Pipeline risk visibility
use_cases.description: Revenue leaders use aggregated conversation data to spot at-risk deals and competitive threats before they show up in forecast slippage.

use_cases.title: CRM-automated call logging
use_cases.description: Reps rely on automatic call summaries and CRM syncing to eliminate manual note-taking after every customer call.
use_cases.audience: Account executives and customer success managers

enrichment.short_description: Atlassian's project and issue tracking software for agile software teams, with Scrum and Kanban boards.
enrichment.seo_title: Jira Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Jira review 2026: explore Atlassian's agile project and issue tracking tool, its Scrum and Kanban boards, AI features, pricing tiers and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Jira is Atlassian's flagship project and issue tracking software, originally built for software development teams and now used broadly across product, IT, marketing and operations teams for planning, tracking and reporting on work. It was the first product built by Atlassian, founded in 2002 in Sydney, Australia by University of New South Wales classmates Mike Cannon-Brookes and Scott Farquhar, who famously bootstrapped the company using 10,000 dollars of credit card debt rather than taking venture funding in its early years. Atlassian went public on NASDAQ in December 2015 under the ticker TEAM, is globally headquartered in Sydney with US headquarters in San Francisco, and as of 2026 employs a workforce that has fluctuated between roughly 12,000 and 14,000 people worldwide following a reported workforce reduction of around 10 percent in March 2026; the company serves more than 300,000 customers across 200-plus countries and territories, with Jira remaining one of its two most widely known products alongside Confluence. Architecturally, Jira Cloud is a multi-tenant SaaS product accessible via web browser and native iOS and Android apps, built around configurable issue types, workflows and permission schemes; Jira Software (the flagship SKU) is purpose-built for agile development with Scrum boards supporting sprint planning, burndown charts and velocity tracking, and Kanban boards supporting WIP limits and cumulative flow diagrams, alongside a backlog for prioritizing unscheduled work and Advanced Roadmaps for coordinating multiple teams on interconnected timelines. Atlassian has layered AI throughout the product via Atlassian Intelligence and Rovo AI agents, which can draft issues from natural-language prompts, summarize long comment threads, suggest automation rules, and answer questions about project status. Automation (built on the no-code Automation for Jira engine) lets teams trigger actions like status changes, assignments or notifications based on rules, with monthly automation-run allowances that scale by plan: 100 runs on Free, 1,700 on Standard, 1,000 per user on Premium, and unlimited on Enterprise. Jira integrates natively with the rest of the Atlassian suite (Confluence, Bitbucket, Compass) as well as with thousands of third-party apps via the Atlassian Marketplace, and connects to CI/CD tools such as Jenkins, CircleCI and Bitbucket Pipelines. Pricing in 2026 follows a per-user-tier model rather than exact per-seat billing: Free supports up to 10 users with core issue tracking, boards and 2GB storage; Standard costs 7.91 dollars per user per month for up to 300 users; Premium costs 14.54 dollars per user per month and adds Advanced Roadmaps, unlimited storage and 24/7 support; and Enterprise is available only on annual contracts with custom pricing, adding org-wide security controls and unlimited automation. Atlassian is retiring self-managed Jira Data Center, ending new license sales on March 30, 2026 with end-of-life set for March 28, 2029, pushing customers toward Cloud or Enterprise Cloud. Jira's core users are software development and product teams practicing Scrum or Kanban, though IT, marketing and operations teams increasingly adopt it for general work tracking. Its primary competitors include Asana, Monday.com, ClickUp, Linear (which has gained significant traction among developer-first teams seeking a lighter-weight alternative), GitHub Issues and Azure DevOps, along with Atlassian's own Trello for simpler use cases; Jira differentiates through its depth of configurability, agile-native reporting, and its position at the center of the broader Atlassian ecosystem, though that same configurability is often cited as a steeper learning curve than lighter competitors like Linear or Trello.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Jira is Atlassian's project and issue tracking software, built originally for software development teams practicing agile methodologies and now widely used by product, IT, marketing and operations teams for planning and tracking work of any kind. It was Atlassian's first product and remains the company's most recognized offering, underpinning workflows for hundreds of thousands of organizations worldwide.

Founded in 2002 in Sydney, Australia by Mike Cannon-Brookes and Scott Farquhar, Atlassian famously bootstrapped its early years without venture funding before going public on NASDAQ in 2015. Jira today ships as a cloud-first SaaS product with configurable workflows, Scrum and Kanban boards, and increasingly deep AI features layered in through Atlassian Intelligence and Rovo.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Jira's agile toolset centers on Scrum boards for sprint planning, burndown charts and velocity tracking, and Kanban boards for continuous-flow work with WIP limits and cumulative flow diagrams. A shared backlog lets teams prioritize and groom upcoming work before it enters a sprint, while Advanced Roadmaps helps larger organizations coordinate dependencies across multiple teams and projects on a single timeline.

No-code automation lets teams trigger status changes, assignments and notifications based on configurable rules, with usage allowances that scale from 100 monthly runs on the Free plan up to unlimited on Enterprise. Atlassian Intelligence and Rovo AI agents extend this further, drafting issues from natural-language prompts, summarizing long threads, and suggesting new automation rules, while deep integrations with Confluence, Bitbucket and CI/CD tools like Jenkins keep engineering workflows connected end to end.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Jira uses a per-user-tier pricing model. The Free plan supports up to 10 users with core issue tracking, Scrum and Kanban boards, and 2GB of storage; Standard costs 7.91 dollars per user per month for up to 300 users; and Premium costs 14.54 dollars per user per month, adding Advanced Roadmaps, unlimited storage and 24/7 support.

Enterprise pricing is custom, billed only on annual contracts, and adds organization-wide security controls, unlimited automation and centralized administration for large deployments. Atlassian is winding down self-managed Jira Data Center, ending new license sales on March 30, 2026 and reaching end of life on March 28, 2029, which is steering most new and existing customers toward Cloud or Enterprise Cloud plans.

features.title: Scrum Boards
features.description: Sprint planning, burndown charts and velocity tracking purpose-built for teams running agile sprints.

features.title: Kanban Boards
features.description: Continuous-flow visualization with WIP limits and cumulative flow diagrams for teams without fixed sprints.

features.title: Backlog Management
features.description: Centralized backlog for prioritizing and grooming upcoming work before it enters a sprint or workflow.

features.title: Advanced Roadmaps
features.description: Cross-team, cross-project timeline planning for coordinating dependencies at scale.

features.title: No-Code Automation
features.description: Rule-based automation for status changes, assignments and notifications with plan-based monthly run limits.

features.title: Atlassian Intelligence and Rovo AI
features.description: AI agents that draft issues from natural language, summarize threads and suggest automation rules.

features.title: Marketplace and Ecosystem Integrations
features.description: Native links to Confluence and Bitbucket plus thousands of third-party apps via the Atlassian Marketplace.

features.title: Custom Workflows and Permission Schemes
features.description: Highly configurable issue types, workflows and permissions to match complex organizational processes.

pros: Deep configurability supports both simple and highly complex agile workflows
Free plan covers up to 10 users with real Scrum, Kanban and backlog functionality
Tight native integration with Confluence, Bitbucket and the wider Atlassian ecosystem
Mature automation engine with generous free-tier run allowances
Backed by a large, well-funded, publicly traded company with a large app marketplace
cons: Steep learning curve and configuration overhead compared to lighter tools like Linear or Trello
Per-user-tier pricing model can make cost planning less intuitive than flat per-seat pricing
Advanced Roadmaps and unlimited automation are locked behind the Premium tier
Self-managed Data Center customers must migrate to Cloud ahead of the 2029 end-of-life
Can feel heavyweight for small teams that only need simple task tracking

faqs.question: Is Jira free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Jira's Free plan supports up to 10 users with core issue tracking, Scrum and Kanban boards, a shared backlog, and 2GB of storage.

faqs.question: Who owns Jira?
faqs.answer: Jira is developed by Atlassian, a publicly traded company (NASDAQ: TEAM) founded in 2002 in Sydney, Australia by Mike Cannon-Brookes and Scott Farquhar.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Jira Standard and Premium?
faqs.answer: Standard costs 7.91 dollars per user per month and covers core agile features for up to 300 users, while Premium costs 14.54 dollars per user per month and adds Advanced Roadmaps, unlimited storage and 24/7 support.

faqs.question: Does Jira support Kanban and Scrum?
faqs.answer: Yes. Jira natively supports both Scrum boards, with sprints and burndown charts, and Kanban boards, with WIP limits and cumulative flow diagrams.

faqs.question: Is Jira Data Center being discontinued?
faqs.answer: Atlassian is ending new license sales for Jira Data Center on March 30, 2026, with full end of life on March 28, 2029, pushing customers toward Jira Cloud.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to Jira?
faqs.answer: Common Jira alternatives include Asana, Monday.com, ClickUp, Linear, GitHub Issues and Azure DevOps, each varying in configurability, agile depth and ease of use.

use_cases.title: Agile software development
use_cases.description: Engineering teams run sprints, track velocity and manage backlogs using Scrum boards tightly integrated with source control via Bitbucket.

use_cases.title: Cross-team roadmap planning
use_cases.description: Product and engineering leadership use Advanced Roadmaps to coordinate dependencies across multiple teams and releases.

use_cases.title: IT and operations task tracking
use_cases.description: Non-engineering teams use Kanban boards and custom workflows to track requests, tasks and approvals outside of software development.

enrichment.short_description: Atlassian's IT service management platform for incident, change, problem and request management.
enrichment.seo_title: Jira Service Management Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Jira Service Management review 2026: see how Atlassian's ITSM platform handles incidents, changes and requests, plus pricing tiers and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Jira Service Management (JSM) is Atlassian's IT service management platform, covering incident management, change management, problem management, service request handling and a Confluence-powered knowledge base for both internal IT teams and end users. It launched in 2013 as Jira Service Desk, a simpler help-desk product built on top of the core Jira platform, and was substantially rebuilt and rebranded to Jira Service Management on November 9, 2020, adding ITIL-aligned change management, major incident workflows and deep integration with Opsgenie, the on-call alerting and incident response tool Atlassian acquired in 2018. Existing Jira Service Desk customers were migrated automatically at no extra cost as part of the rebrand. As with Jira Software, JSM is developed by Atlassian, founded in 2002 in Sydney, Australia by Mike Cannon-Brookes and Scott Farquhar, publicly traded on NASDAQ under the ticker TEAM since its December 2015 IPO, globally headquartered in Sydney with US headquarters in San Francisco, and employing a workforce that has ranged between roughly 12,000 and 14,000 people through 2026. Atlassian has been consolidating Opsgenie's standalone product directly into JSM: Opsgenie stopped new standalone sales in mid-2025 and will reach full end of support in April 2027, with its on-call scheduling, alerting and incident-swarming capabilities now bundled natively into JSM's cloud editions. Architecturally, JSM runs as a cloud-native SaaS ITSM suite built on the same underlying Jira platform as Jira Software, letting organizations that already use Jira for engineering extend the same instance into IT support without a separate system. Its incident management module aggregates alerts from monitoring, logging and service desk tools, reduces noise through automated deduplication, and routes notifications via SMS, email, push, chat and voice calls using configurable on-call schedules. Change management follows ITIL-style workflows with automated risk assessment, multi-stage approval gates, a change calendar, and CI/CD integrations with Bitbucket Pipelines, Jenkins and CircleCI that let low-risk changes bypass manual approval. The knowledge base, powered by Confluence, gives support teams and self-service end users a shared repository of guides, FAQs and troubleshooting articles, while a customizable customer portal handles service request intake and SLA tracking. Pricing in 2026 spans four tiers: Free, capped at 3 agents, includes core service desk and knowledge base features for very small teams; Standard costs roughly 19 to 24 dollars per agent per month depending on volume tier and covers request, incident and problem management without CMDB or major incident tooling; Premium costs roughly 47 to 55 dollars per agent per month and unlocks ITIL workflows, on-call scheduling via the integrated Opsgenie capabilities, a configuration management database (CMDB), major incident management, AI features and 24/7 support, more than doubling the per-agent cost over Standard; and Enterprise offers custom annual pricing for large, globally distributed organizations with strict governance, data residency and security requirements, typically serving deployments of 500-plus agents. JSM's target customers range from small IT teams needing basic ticketing to enterprise IT operations and DevOps organizations that want incident response tightly coupled to their existing Jira-based engineering workflows. Its principal competitors are ServiceNow, the dominant enterprise ITSM platform; Zendesk and Freshservice, which compete more on ease of use and mid-market pricing; BMC Helix; and ManageEngine ServiceDesk Plus; JSM differentiates primarily through its native integration with Jira Software and Confluence, making it a natural fit for organizations that already run engineering workflows on Atlassian, along with a materially lower entry price than ServiceNow for small and mid-sized IT teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Jira Service Management is Atlassian's IT service management platform, covering incident, change, problem and request management alongside a Confluence-powered knowledge base for internal support teams and end users. It launched in 2013 as the simpler Jira Service Desk before being rebuilt and rebranded to Jira Service Management in November 2020 with deeper ITIL-aligned workflows and Opsgenie integration.

Built on the same underlying platform as Jira Software, JSM lets organizations already using Jira for engineering extend the same instance into IT operations without standing up a separate system, appealing especially to DevOps-oriented teams that want incident response tightly coupled to development workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: JSM's incident management module aggregates alerts from monitoring and logging tools, deduplicates noise, and routes notifications through SMS, email, push, chat and voice calls based on configurable on-call schedules, largely powered by capabilities absorbed from Opsgenie. Change management follows ITIL-style workflows with automated risk scoring, multi-stage approvals, a change calendar, and CI/CD integrations that let low-risk changes skip manual review entirely.

A Confluence-powered knowledge base gives both support agents and self-service end users access to guides, FAQs and troubleshooting articles, reducing repetitive ticket volume, while a configurable customer portal manages service request intake and SLA tracking. Premium and Enterprise tiers add a configuration management database (CMDB) and major incident management for coordinating cross-team responses to significant outages.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: JSM offers four tiers. Free is capped at 3 agents and covers core request and knowledge base functionality; Standard runs roughly 19 to 24 dollars per agent per month depending on volume and adds incident and problem management without CMDB or major incident tooling.

Premium runs roughly 47 to 55 dollars per agent per month and unlocks ITIL workflows, integrated on-call scheduling, CMDB, major incident management, AI features and 24/7 support, more than doubling the per-agent price over Standard. Enterprise is custom-priced and billed annually, aimed at large organizations with strict governance and security requirements, typically at deployments of 500 or more agents.

features.title: Incident Management
features.description: Aggregates and deduplicates alerts from monitoring tools, then routes on-call notifications via SMS, chat, email or voice.

features.title: Change Management
features.description: ITIL-style change workflows with automated risk assessment, approval gates and a shared change calendar.

features.title: Confluence-Powered Knowledge Base
features.description: Shared repository of guides, FAQs and troubleshooting articles for agents and self-service end users.

features.title: Customer Service Portal
features.description: Configurable self-service portal for submitting and tracking service requests against SLAs.

features.title: Major Incident Management
features.description: Coordinates cross-team response workflows for significant, high-impact outages (Premium and above).

features.title: Configuration Management Database (CMDB)
features.description: Tracks IT assets and their relationships to support impact analysis during incidents and changes.

features.title: On-Call Scheduling
features.description: Opsgenie-derived on-call rotations and escalation policies built natively into JSM's cloud editions.

features.title: CI/CD Integrations
features.description: Connects to Bitbucket Pipelines, Jenkins and CircleCI so low-risk deployments can bypass manual approval.

pros: Native integration with Jira Software and Confluence suits teams already on the Atlassian ecosystem
Significantly lower entry price than enterprise ITSM leaders like ServiceNow
Opsgenie's on-call alerting and incident response are now bundled into higher tiers at no extra cost
Free tier available for very small IT teams up to 3 agents
Strong DevOps alignment through CI/CD integrations for automated, low-risk change approvals
cons: Premium tier more than doubles the per-agent cost over Standard to unlock CMDB and major incident management
Standalone Opsgenie is being phased out, requiring customers to migrate on Atlassian's timeline
Less mature enterprise ITSM depth than ServiceNow for very large, complex IT organizations
Per-agent, volume-tiered pricing can be difficult to forecast as agent counts scale
Enterprise tier requires annual contracts and custom sales negotiation, with no public pricing

faqs.question: What is Jira Service Management used for?
faqs.answer: Jira Service Management is an ITSM platform used for incident management, change management, problem management, service request handling and internal or customer-facing knowledge bases.

faqs.question: Is Jira Service Management the same as Jira Service Desk?
faqs.answer: Jira Service Desk was rebranded and substantially rebuilt into Jira Service Management on November 9, 2020, with existing customers migrated automatically at no extra cost.

faqs.question: How much does Jira Service Management cost?
faqs.answer: JSM has a Free plan for up to 3 agents, a Standard plan around 19 to 24 dollars per agent per month, a Premium plan around 47 to 55 dollars per agent per month, and custom Enterprise pricing.

faqs.question: Is Opsgenie included in Jira Service Management?
faqs.answer: Opsgenie's on-call scheduling and alerting capabilities are being integrated natively into JSM's cloud editions, as standalone Opsgenie is being phased out with full end of support in April 2027.

faqs.question: Does Jira Service Management include a CMDB?
faqs.answer: Yes, a configuration management database is available starting on the Premium plan, letting teams track IT assets and their relationships during incidents and changes.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to Jira Service Management?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include ServiceNow, Zendesk, Freshservice, BMC Helix and ManageEngine ServiceDesk Plus, which vary in enterprise depth, ease of use and pricing.

use_cases.title: IT help desk and service requests
use_cases.description: IT teams manage employee support tickets, SLAs and a self-service knowledge base through a configurable customer portal.

use_cases.title: DevOps incident response
use_cases.description: Engineering and SRE teams use integrated on-call scheduling and alert routing tied directly to the same Jira instance used for development work.
use_cases.audience: DevOps and site reliability engineering teams

use_cases.title: Enterprise change management
use_cases.description: Large organizations use ITIL-aligned change workflows with automated risk scoring and CI/CD integration to govern production deployments.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source video conferencing platform with no account required, stewarded by 8x8.
enrichment.seo_title: Jitsi Meet Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Jitsi Meet review 2026: explore this free, open-source, self-hostable video conferencing platform, its encryption, features, pricing and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Jitsi Meet is a free, open-source video conferencing platform that runs directly in a web browser without requiring an account, download, or payment, and can also be fully self-hosted by organizations that want complete control over their infrastructure and data. Its roots trace to 2003, when Emil Ivov started an academic project called SIP Communicator at the University of Strasbourg in France focused on secure, open standards-based VoIP communication; in 2011 the project was rebranded Jitsi, derived from the Bulgarian word for wires, and Ivov founded BlueJimp to commercialize and maintain it. Atlassian acquired Jitsi and BlueJimp in 2015 with plans to add video calling to its HipChat and later Stride chat products, but as those products lost ground to Slack, Atlassian sold the Jitsi technology and its engineering team, still led by Emil Ivov, to cloud communications provider 8x8 in October 2018, with 8x8 publicly committing to keep the project open source. That commitment held: Jitsi Meet remains released under the Apache 2.0 license, with its source code, including the videobridge (JVB) selective forwarding unit, conference focus (Jicofo) and web client, publicly available on GitHub. During the COVID-19 pandemic, usage of the free public instance at meet.jit.si spiked dramatically, with 8x8 reporting over 10.4 million monthly active users by April 2020. 8x8 itself is a publicly traded (NASDAQ: EGHT) cloud communications company headquartered in Campbell, California, with roughly 2,000 employees worldwide as of 2025-2026. Architecturally, Jitsi Meet uses WebRTC for real-time audio and video, with a selective forwarding unit architecture that lets it scale group calls more efficiently than simple mesh networking; it supports optional end-to-end encryption that, when enabled, prevents even the server operator from decrypting media streams, alongside standard transport encryption for all calls by default. The platform supports screen sharing, YouTube live streaming, virtual backgrounds, breakout rooms, live captions, chat, and integrations with calendar tools; it is distributed as a free public web service, downloadable native apps for Windows, Mac, Linux, iOS and Android, and self-hostable Docker or native Linux packages for organizations with data residency or compliance requirements. For organizations that want managed infrastructure without self-hosting, 8x8 sells Jitsi as a Service (JaaS), a commercial API and SDK offering priced on a monthly-active-user basis starting at 0.35 dollars per MAU with volume discounts, including a free developer tier for up to 25 monthly active users, plus an additional 0.01 dollars per minute for cloud recording. Jitsi Meet's core audience spans privacy-conscious individuals, open-source advocates, educational institutions, and developers who want to embed video calling into their own applications via JaaS rather than build WebRTC infrastructure from scratch; several universities and community organizations, including Goethe University Frankfurt, run their own self-hosted Jitsi deployments. Its principal competitors are Zoom and Google Meet, both of which dominate the market but require accounts and route data through proprietary, closed infrastructure, along with other open-source or privacy-focused alternatives such as BigBlueButton and Jami. Jitsi Meet differentiates through the unmatched combination of zero cost, no account requirement, unlimited call duration on the free public instance, full self-hosting capability, and permissive open-source licensing, trading some of the polish and enterprise administrative tooling of Zoom for transparency and infrastructure control.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Jitsi Meet is a free, open-source video conferencing platform that runs in a web browser without requiring an account or download, and can be fully self-hosted for organizations that want complete control over their video infrastructure and data. It grew out of a 2003 academic VoIP project called SIP Communicator, was rebranded Jitsi in 2011, and has been owned and stewarded by cloud communications provider 8x8 since 2018 after a brief period under Atlassian.

Unlike closed platforms such as Zoom or Google Meet, Jitsi Meet's full source code, including its videobridge, conference focus and web client, is published under the Apache 2.0 license on GitHub, letting anyone audit, modify or self-host the software. This openness, combined with a free public instance at meet.jit.si, made Jitsi a popular privacy-conscious alternative during the pandemic-era surge in video conferencing usage.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Jitsi Meet is built on WebRTC with a selective forwarding unit architecture that scales group calls more efficiently than simple peer-to-peer meshes, and supports optional end-to-end encryption that prevents even the server operator from accessing meeting content when enabled. Standard features include screen sharing, YouTube live streaming, virtual backgrounds, breakout rooms, live captions and in-meeting chat.

The platform ships as a free public web service, native desktop and mobile apps for Windows, Mac, Linux, iOS and Android, and self-hostable Docker or native Linux packages for organizations with strict data residency or compliance needs. Developers who want to embed video calling into their own products can use 8x8's Jitsi as a Service (JaaS), a hosted API and SDK layer built on the same open-source core.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The core Jitsi Meet software is free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license, with no cost for using the public meet.jit.si instance or for self-hosting your own deployment, aside from the infrastructure and technical staff time required to run and maintain it.

For teams that want managed hosting instead of self-hosting, 8x8 offers Jitsi as a Service (JaaS) on a monthly-active-user pricing model starting at 0.35 dollars per MAU with volume discounts, including a free developer tier covering up to 25 monthly active users; cloud call recording is billed separately at 0.01 dollars per minute.

features.title: No Account Required
features.description: Anyone can start or join a meeting on the free public instance without creating an account or installing software.

features.title: End-to-End Encryption
features.description: Optional E2EE ensures only meeting participants, not even the server operator, can decrypt audio and video streams.

features.title: Self-Hosting
features.description: Fully open-source Docker and native Linux deployment options give organizations complete control over their infrastructure and data.

features.title: Screen Sharing and Live Streaming
features.description: Built-in screen sharing plus direct YouTube live streaming for webinars and public broadcasts.

features.title: Breakout Rooms
features.description: Meeting hosts can split participants into smaller group sessions for workshops and classes.

features.title: Cross-Platform Apps
features.description: Native apps for Windows, Mac, Linux, iOS and Android alongside full browser support.

features.title: Live Captions
features.description: Real-time captioning support for accessibility during meetings.

features.title: Jitsi as a Service (JaaS)
features.description: Managed API and SDK from 8x8 for embedding Jitsi's video infrastructure into third-party applications, billed per monthly active user.

pros: Completely free with no account, download or payment required for the public instance
Fully open source under Apache 2.0, allowing complete self-hosting and code auditing
Optional end-to-end encryption that even the server operator cannot decrypt
No hard cap on meeting duration or participant count on self-hosted deployments
Backed by 8x8, a publicly traded communications company, ensuring ongoing maintenance
cons: Call quality can degrade on the free public instance with large groups (roughly 35-plus participants)
Fewer built-in enterprise administration and compliance tools than Zoom or Microsoft Teams
Self-hosting requires technical expertise and ongoing server maintenance
Mobile and desktop app polish lags behind well-funded commercial competitors
JaaS commercial pricing and recording add-ons are less transparent than flat-rate competitor plans

faqs.question: Is Jitsi Meet completely free?
faqs.answer: Yes. The public meet.jit.si instance is free to use with no account required, and the underlying software is free and open source for self-hosting.

faqs.question: Who owns Jitsi Meet?
faqs.answer: Jitsi Meet is stewarded by 8x8, Inc., a publicly traded cloud communications company that acquired the Jitsi technology and engineering team from Atlassian in October 2018.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Jitsi Meet?
faqs.answer: Yes. Jitsi Meet's source code is released under the Apache 2.0 license and can be deployed on your own servers via Docker or native Linux packages for full data control.

faqs.question: Does Jitsi Meet support end-to-end encryption?
faqs.answer: Yes. Jitsi Meet offers optional end-to-end encryption that, when enabled, prevents even the server operator from decrypting meeting audio and video.

faqs.question: Do I need an account to join a Jitsi Meet call?
faqs.answer: No. Participants can join meetings on the public instance directly from a link in their browser without creating an account.

faqs.question: What is Jitsi as a Service (JaaS)?
faqs.answer: JaaS is 8x8's managed, commercial API and SDK offering built on the open-source Jitsi core, priced per monthly active user starting at 0.35 dollars, aimed at developers embedding video calling into their own products.

use_cases.title: Privacy-conscious personal and team calls
use_cases.description: Users who want to avoid mandatory accounts and proprietary infrastructure use the free public instance for everyday video calls.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted institutional video conferencing
use_cases.description: Universities and community organizations deploy self-hosted Jitsi instances to keep meeting data on infrastructure they control.

use_cases.title: Embedded video calling for developers
use_cases.description: Product teams use Jitsi as a Service to add video calling to their own applications without building WebRTC infrastructure from scratch.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source data ingestion engine and Segment alternative for building real-time customer data pipelines.
enrichment.seo_title: Jitsu Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Jitsu review 2026: see how this open-source Segment alternative handles real-time data ingestion, self-hosting, pricing tiers and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Jitsu is an open-source data ingestion and event collection engine built as a self-hostable alternative to Segment, letting engineering and data teams set up real-time customer data pipelines that route events from websites and apps into warehouses, analytics tools and downstream destinations. The company was founded in 2019 by Peter Wysinski and Vladimir Klimontovich, both of whom had previously worked as CTOs and started an earlier open-source project called EventNative that formed the technical basis for Jitsu; the company is based in Berkeley, California, and went through the Y Combinator Summer 2020 batch, publicly launching on Hacker News as 'Jitsu (YC S20): Open-Source Segment Alternative.' In December 2020, Jitsu raised 2 million dollars in seed funding led by Costanoa Ventures, with participation from Y Combinator, The House Fund and SignalFire, funding to build out its open-core data integration platform. Architecturally, Jitsu is built as a fully-scriptable, cloud-native data ingestion engine: it captures events from web and mobile SDKs or server-side API calls, applies configurable JavaScript or YAML-based transformations in real time, and routes the resulting data to a growing library of connectors and destinations, including data warehouses like BigQuery, Snowflake, Redshift and ClickHouse, as well as marketing and analytics tools. The product ships with a built-in events debugger for troubleshooting pipeline issues, unlimited destinations even on lower tiers, and support for both scheduled batch connector syncs and low-latency streaming for time-sensitive use cases. The full codebase is released under the MIT license and available on GitHub, and can be deployed via Docker on any infrastructure the customer controls, giving privacy- and cost-conscious teams a genuine self-hosting path rather than a crippled free trial. Commercially, Jitsu follows an open-core model with four tiers: a fully free, self-hosted Open Source tier with unlimited events (bounded only by the customer's own infrastructure) and community support; a hosted Free plan on Jitsu Cloud that includes 200,000 events per month, unlimited connectors on manual sync only, and Clickhouse-backed analytics access; a Business plan priced at 99 dollars per month that raises the included volume to 2 million events per month (with overage at 40 dollars per additional million events), enables up to 5 active syncs and hourly sync frequency, and is available across Jitsu Cloud, Private Cloud or on-prem deployment; and a custom-priced Enterprise plan offering unlimited monthly active syncs, one-minute sync frequency, and dedicated deployment options for large-scale data teams. Jitsu's target customers are engineering and data teams at startups and mid-market companies who want Segment-like customer data infrastructure without Segment's per-event enterprise pricing or its closed-source architecture, as well as privacy- and compliance-driven organizations that need to keep raw event data on infrastructure they control. Its most direct competitor is Segment itself (owned by Twilio), the category-defining commercial customer data platform; other alternatives include RudderStack, another open-source, self-hostable Segment competitor with a very similar positioning, along with Snowplow and PostHog for teams whose primary need is product analytics rather than pure data routing. Jitsu differentiates from Segment primarily on cost and control, offering a genuinely free, unlimited self-hosted tier and transparent usage-based cloud pricing, while differentiating from closer open-source rival RudderStack mainly on its lighter-weight, developer-first configuration model built around YAML and JavaScript transformations rather than a heavier enterprise-oriented feature set.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Jitsu is an open-source data ingestion engine positioned as a self-hostable alternative to Segment, letting engineering and data teams collect events from websites and apps and route them in real time to data warehouses, analytics platforms and marketing tools. It was founded in 2019 by Peter Wysinski and Vladimir Klimontovich, went through Y Combinator's Summer 2020 batch, and is based in Berkeley, California.

The project's core differentiator is that its full codebase is released under the MIT license and can be self-hosted via Docker on infrastructure the customer controls, giving teams a genuine free option rather than a limited trial of a closed commercial product, while still offering managed cloud hosting for teams that prefer not to run their own infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Jitsu captures events from web and mobile SDKs or direct API calls, applies configurable JavaScript or YAML-based transformations in real time, and routes the resulting data to destinations including BigQuery, Snowflake, Redshift and ClickHouse alongside marketing and analytics tools. A built-in events debugger helps engineering teams troubleshoot pipeline issues without digging through raw logs.

The platform supports both connector-based batch syncs, with configurable sync frequency ranging from daily to as fast as one minute on higher tiers, and lower-latency streaming ingestion for time-sensitive use cases. Deployment flexibility spans Jitsu Cloud, a customer-managed Private Cloud, and fully on-prem installations, letting teams choose the tradeoff between operational simplicity and infrastructure control.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Jitsu's Open Source tier is free forever for self-hosted deployments, limited only by the customer's own infrastructure, and includes the full MIT-licensed codebase with community support. A hosted Free plan on Jitsu Cloud includes 200,000 events per month with unlimited connectors on manual sync only.

The Business plan costs 99 dollars per month, raising the included volume to 2 million events monthly (with overage billed at 40 dollars per additional million events), enabling up to 5 active syncs and hourly sync frequency across Cloud, Private Cloud or on-prem deployment. An Enterprise plan offers custom pricing with unlimited monthly active syncs and one-minute sync frequency for large-scale data teams.

features.title: Real-Time Event Ingestion
features.description: Captures events from web and mobile SDKs or server-side API calls and routes them to destinations with low latency.

features.title: Scriptable Transformations
features.description: Configurable JavaScript or YAML-based transformations let teams reshape event data in-flight before it reaches a destination.

features.title: Data Warehouse Connectors
features.description: Native destinations include BigQuery, Snowflake, Redshift and ClickHouse for downstream analytics and modeling.

features.title: Events Debugger
features.description: Built-in debugging tool for tracing individual events through the pipeline to troubleshoot delivery issues.

features.title: Self-Hosting via Docker
features.description: Full MIT-licensed codebase can be deployed on customer-controlled infrastructure for cost and compliance reasons.

features.title: Unlimited Destinations
features.description: Even lower-cost tiers include unlimited destination connections rather than gating by destination count.

features.title: Flexible Sync Scheduling
features.description: Connector sync frequency ranges from manual and daily on lower tiers up to one-minute intervals on Enterprise.

pros: Genuinely free, unlimited self-hosted open-source tier under the MIT license
Transparent, usage-based cloud pricing compared to enterprise-negotiated competitors like Segment
Flexible deployment across Cloud, Private Cloud and on-prem for compliance-sensitive teams
Developer-friendly configuration via JavaScript and YAML transformations
Backed by Y Combinator and venture funding, supporting continued development
cons: Smaller connector and integration library than category leader Segment
Self-hosting requires engineering resources to deploy, scale and maintain the infrastructure
Business plan overage pricing (40 dollars per additional million events) can add up for high-volume sites
Smaller company and community than more heavily funded competitors, which may affect long-term roadmap pace
Enterprise-grade governance and support tooling is less mature than established commercial CDPs

faqs.question: Is Jitsu really free?
faqs.answer: Yes. Jitsu's Open Source tier is free forever for self-hosted deployments under the MIT license, and a hosted Free plan on Jitsu Cloud includes 200,000 events per month.

faqs.question: Who founded Jitsu?
faqs.answer: Jitsu was founded in 2019 by Peter Wysinski and Vladimir Klimontovich, who previously built an open-source project called EventNative, and the company went through Y Combinator's Summer 2020 batch.

faqs.question: How is Jitsu different from Segment?
faqs.answer: Jitsu is open source and self-hostable under the MIT license with transparent usage-based pricing, while Segment is a closed-source, enterprise-priced commercial customer data platform owned by Twilio.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Jitsu?
faqs.answer: Yes. Jitsu's full codebase is open source and can be deployed via Docker on infrastructure you control, on-prem or in a private cloud.

faqs.question: What data warehouses does Jitsu support?
faqs.answer: Jitsu connects natively to data warehouses including BigQuery, Snowflake, Redshift and ClickHouse, along with a range of marketing and analytics destinations.

faqs.question: What does the Jitsu Business plan cost?
faqs.answer: The Business plan costs 99 dollars per month, including 2 million events monthly, up to 5 active syncs and hourly sync frequency, with additional events billed at 40 dollars per million.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted customer data pipelines
use_cases.description: Engineering teams deploy Jitsu on their own infrastructure to collect and route event data while keeping raw data under their own control.
use_cases.audience: Data engineering teams with compliance requirements

use_cases.title: Segment cost replacement
use_cases.description: Startups and mid-market companies migrate from Segment to Jitsu Cloud to cut per-event costs while keeping a similar data pipeline architecture.

use_cases.title: Warehouse-first analytics
use_cases.description: Teams route web and app events directly into BigQuery, Snowflake or ClickHouse to power internal analytics and BI tools.

enrichment.short_description: Browser-based motion design tool for creating animations and micro-interactions, often called Figma for motion.
enrichment.seo_title: Jitter Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Jitter review 2026: explore this browser-based motion design tool's Figma import, AI features, templates, pricing plans and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Jitter is a browser-based motion design tool that lets designers, marketers and social media teams create animations, micro-interactions and short motion graphics without traditional video-editing or animation software, often described in the design community as a 'Figma for motion' because of its collaborative, infinite-canvas interface and Figma-native import workflow. The company was founded in 2020 by Sebastien Robaszkiewicz, who serves as CEO and previously worked in product design, and Etienne Albert, who serves as CTO and had previously co-founded Stupeflix, a mobile video-editing startup acquired by GoPro. Jitter is headquartered in the Paris region of France (registered in Vincennes, with its team based in Paris), and went through Y Combinator's Winter 2020 batch, raising 150,000 dollars in seed funding through the accelerator; the team has remained deliberately small, reported at around 7 employees, reflecting its focus on a tightly scoped, highly polished product rather than a broad feature sprawl. Architecturally, Jitter runs entirely in the browser as a web application with no desktop install required, built around an infinite canvas, real-time multiplayer collaboration similar to Figma, and a direct import pipeline that lets designers bring existing Figma frames and layers straight into Jitter to animate them, preserving vector shapes, text and component structure rather than requiring a re-build from scratch. The product ships with a large library of more than 300 searchable motion graphics templates covering categories like social media posts, logo reveals, product demos and kinetic typography, along with a set of one-click animation presets, curved-path motion, custom vector shape morphing, multi-stop gradient animation and animated blur effects. In 2026 the company introduced Jitter AI, an AI-assisted layer that can turn static images into subtly animated video, generate multiple design and motion variations from a single starting point with one click, and respond to natural-language prompts (branded 'Superagents') to remix templates or animate complex scenes from scratch.
