Pricing follows a freemium structure with five tiers: a Free plan limited to 3 workspace files, 720p export resolution and 30fps with a Jitter watermark; a Pro plan (reported at roughly 19 dollars per editor per month, billed annually) for individuals and freelancers that removes the watermark, unlocks unlimited files and raises export to 1080p at 60fps; a Max plan, the most popular paid tier, aimed at teams, which raises export to 4K at 120fps and adds fuller collaboration features; an Ultra plan built specifically around AI workflows, providing the highest monthly allotment of AI credits for high-volume motion generation; and a custom-priced Enterprise plan for large organizations, adding advanced security, custom SSO and SCIM provisioning, and dedicated account management. All paid plans are billed annually with a reported 15 percent discount versus monthly billing. Jitter's target audience is design and marketing teams that need fast, lightweight motion content, such as animated social posts, product marketing clips and UI micro-interaction previews, without the steep learning curve of full-featured tools like After Effects. Its principal competitors are Adobe After Effects, the long-established industry-standard motion graphics tool aimed at professional motion designers and video editors; Rive, which focuses on interactive, code-integrated animations for app and game interfaces; Canva, which offers simpler templated animation as part of a broader design suite; and Figma itself, which has added some native prototyping and animation capabilities. Jitter differentiates from these rivals through its narrow focus on fast, browser-native motion design with direct Figma interoperability, trading the deep compositing and effects power of After Effects for dramatically faster time-to-output and a gentler learning curve suited to designers rather than dedicated motion specialists.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Jitter is a browser-based motion design tool that lets designers and marketers create animations and micro-interactions without traditional video-editing software, often compared to Figma because of its collaborative, infinite-canvas workflow and native Figma import support. It was founded in 2020 by Sebastien Robaszkiewicz and Etienne Albert, who previously co-founded a video-editing startup acquired by GoPro, and the company went through Y Combinator's Winter 2020 batch.

Based near Paris, France, Jitter has stayed intentionally small, with a team of around 7 people focused on a tightly scoped product rather than broad feature sprawl. Its core appeal is speed: designers can import existing Figma layers directly into Jitter, apply animation presets or AI-assisted variations, and export polished motion graphics in minutes rather than hours.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Jitter's defining feature is its Figma import pipeline, which preserves vector shapes, text and component structure when bringing designs in from Figma, letting designers animate existing UI or brand assets without rebuilding them. The infinite canvas supports real-time multiplayer collaboration, and the platform ships with over 300 searchable templates spanning social posts, logo reveals, product demos and kinetic typography, plus one-click animation presets, curved-path motion and custom shape morphing.

In 2026, Jitter introduced Jitter AI, which can turn static images into subtly animated video, generate multiple motion variations from a single starting design with one click, and respond to natural-language prompts through a feature branded Superagents to remix templates or build complex animated scenes from scratch, extending the product beyond manual keyframe editing into AI-assisted motion generation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Jitter's Free plan is limited to 3 workspace files, 720p export and 30fps with a watermark. The Pro plan, aimed at individuals and freelancers and reported at roughly 19 dollars per editor per month billed annually, removes the watermark and unlocks unlimited files with 1080p export at 60fps.

The Max plan, Jitter's most popular paid tier, targets teams with 4K export at 120fps and fuller collaboration tools, while the Ultra plan is built around AI workflows with the highest monthly allotment of AI credits. An Enterprise plan offers custom pricing with advanced security, SSO, SCIM provisioning and dedicated account management for large organizations, with all paid plans billed annually at a reported 15 percent discount versus monthly billing.

features.title: Figma Import
features.description: Direct import of Figma frames and layers into Jitter while preserving vector shapes, text and component structure.

features.title: Infinite Canvas Collaboration
features.description: Real-time, multiplayer editing on an infinite canvas similar to Figma's collaborative workflow.

features.title: Motion Graphics Template Library
features.description: Over 300 searchable templates covering social posts, logo reveals, product demos and kinetic typography.

features.title: Animation Presets
features.description: One-click presets plus curved-path motion, custom vector shape morphing and multi-stop gradient animation.

features.title: Jitter AI
features.description: AI-assisted tools that animate static images, generate design and motion variations, and respond to natural-language prompts to build scenes.

features.title: High-Resolution Export
features.description: Export up to 4K resolution at 120fps on higher-tier plans, suitable for professional marketing and product use.

features.title: Team Libraries
features.description: Shared component and template libraries for consistent brand motion assets across a team (Max and above).

pros: Direct Figma import dramatically speeds up animating existing design assets
Fully browser-based with no software install and strong real-time collaboration
Large, searchable template library covering common social and marketing motion formats
Jitter AI adds one-click variation generation and prompt-based scene creation
Much gentler learning curve than professional tools like Adobe After Effects
cons: Free plan is limited to 3 files, 720p export and a visible watermark
Less deep compositing, effects and control than dedicated professional tools like After Effects
Team libraries and full collaboration features are gated behind the Max tier and above
AI credit allotments on lower tiers may not suit high-volume motion production workflows
Small team size relative to larger design-tool competitors could affect release pace over time

faqs.question: What is Jitter used for?
faqs.answer: Jitter is a browser-based motion design tool for creating animations, micro-interactions and short motion graphics, commonly used for social media content, product marketing and UI animation previews.

faqs.question: Can I import Figma designs into Jitter?
faqs.answer: Yes. Jitter has a direct Figma import pipeline that preserves vector shapes, text and component structure, letting designers animate existing Figma assets without rebuilding them.

faqs.question: Is Jitter free to use?
faqs.answer: Jitter offers a Free plan limited to 3 workspace files, 720p export and 30fps with a watermark; paid Pro, Max and Ultra plans remove these limits and add higher-resolution export and AI features.

faqs.question: What is Jitter AI?
faqs.answer: Jitter AI is an AI-assisted layer introduced in 2026 that can animate static images, generate one-click design and motion variations, and respond to natural-language prompts to build or remix animated scenes.

faqs.question: Who founded Jitter?
faqs.answer: Jitter was founded in 2020 by Sebastien Robaszkiewicz (CEO) and Etienne Albert (CTO), who previously co-founded the video-editing startup Stupeflix, acquired by GoPro.

faqs.question: How is Jitter different from Adobe After Effects?
faqs.answer: Jitter is a lightweight, browser-based tool focused on fast, template-driven motion graphics and Figma interoperability, while After Effects is a professional desktop application with far deeper compositing and effects capabilities aimed at dedicated motion designers.

use_cases.title: Social media animation
use_cases.description: Marketing teams use Jitter's templates and presets to quickly produce animated social posts and ad creative without a video editor.

use_cases.title: Product and UI micro-interaction previews
use_cases.description: Product designers animate imported Figma components to prototype and present micro-interactions to stakeholders and engineers.

use_cases.title: Brand and logo motion
use_cases.description: Freelancers and agencies use Jitter's shape morphing and gradient animation tools to build animated logo reveals and brand assets for clients.
